original_abstract:
Offloading computationally intensive tasks from mobile devices to high-performance cloud servers is a compelling strategy for enhancing mobile performance while extending battery life. This paper investigates a multi-cell MIMO system where multiple mobile users (MUs) seek to offload their workloads to a shared cloud environment. We model this as a joint optimization problem, managing both radio resources—specifically the transmit precoding matrices—and computational resources, defined as the CPU cycles per second allocated by the cloud to each user. Our objective is to minimize total energy consumption across all devices while strictly adhering to latency requirements.

The resulting mathematical formulation is inherently non-convex in both the objective function and the constraints, presenting a significant computational challenge. Despite this complexity, we derive a closed-form global optimal solution for the single-user case. For the more complex multi-user scenario, we develop an iterative algorithm based on a novel successive convex approximation (SCA) technique that ensures convergence to a locally optimal solution. A key feature of this framework is its suitability for distributed and parallel implementation across radio access points, requiring only minimal coordination with the cloud. Numerical simulations demonstrate that our joint optimization approach significantly outperforms traditional disjoint resource allocation schemes.
humanized_abstract:
A Cloud-Based Resource Allocation Method for Mobile Devices to Achieve Performance Enhancement and Battery Extension Offloading the computationally intensive tasks from mobile devices to cloud servers via the network is a popular method to achieve mobile performance improvement and battery extension. In this paper, we study the multi-cell MIMO system where multiple mobile users (MUs) want to offload their tasks to a cloud. This problem is formulated as a joint resource allocation problem in terms of transmit precoding matrices at the base stations and CPU cycles per second at the cloud side for each MU. Our goal is to minimize the total energy consumption of all MUs and bases under given latency constraints.

The resulting mathematical optimization problem is non-convex, both in terms of the objective and constraints. Optimal solution is derived in a closed form for the single-user case. An iterative successive convex approximation based algorithm is then proposed to tackle the multi-user case. Thanks to a specially design global lower bound approximation, the iterative algorithm is shown to converge to a local optimum. Interestingly, each of the variables for each RAN can be distributedly and/or in parallel processed at each RAN with almost no requirement for central cloud exchange. Simulations show that the proposed joint optimization scheme clearly yields much better performance compared to disjoint optimization.

Offloading computationally intensive tasks from mobile devices to high-performance cloud servers is a compelling strategy for enhancing mobile performance while extending battery life.
